title: Starting Long-Term Oral Corticosteroids: Prednisone Therapy & Self-Management Teaching
subtitle: A 64-year-old woman is started on long-term prednisone for polymyalgia rheumatica. The nurse teaches safe corticosteroid use — never stopping abruptly, sick-day stress dosing, glucose and infection monitoring, and bone protection.

patient.name: Margaret Boyd
patient.admitDate: Rheumatology clinic. New polymyalgia rheumatica started on prednisone 20 mg daily with a planned slow taper. PMH borderline glucose intolerance, HTN, osteopenia. Here for medication teaching before going home.

labs.r: Watch GI bleeding, esp. with NSAIDs.

nursesNote: Margaret Boyd, 64, starting long-term prednisone for polymyalgia rheumatica with a planned taper. Early effects: glucose 168, K 3.3, BP rising with +2 kg and mild edema; osteopenia on DEXA. Chronic use suppresses the HPA axis. Teaching focus: never stop abruptly, take in the morning with food, sick-day stress dosing, infection precautions, glucose/BP/weight monitoring, bone protection, no live vaccines, and a medical-alert ID.
providerOrders: Prednisone per taper schedule; do NOT discontinue abruptly
Monitor glucose, BP, weight, and potassium
Start calcium + vitamin D; bone-protection per guidelines
Teach infection precautions, sick-day stress dosing, and medical-alert ID
Avoid live vaccines; review and limit concurrent NSAIDs

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What must the patient understand about long-term steroids?

opts.text: Long-term corticosteroids suppress the HPA axis — never stop them abruptly
Abrupt withdrawal can trigger adrenal crisis (hypotension, weakness, nausea)
Steroids raise blood glucose and can cause hyperglycemia
They increase infection risk and can MASK fever and other signs
It is fine to stop prednisone as soon as she feels better
Long-term use risks osteoporosis, weight gain, and mood/sleep changes

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How should the drug be taken and monitored?

opts.text: Take the dose in the MORNING with food to mimic cortisol rhythm and ease GI upset
Doses must be TAPERED, never stopped suddenly
During illness or surgery the body needs MORE steroid (stress dosing)
Hyperglycemia, hypokalemia, fluid retention, and rising BP are expected effects
A live vaccine is preferred while taking immunosuppressive steroids
New black, tarry stools could mean GI bleeding and must be reported

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the teaching and monitoring priorities

opts.text: Confirm she understands NEVER to stop the steroid abruptly
Teach correct dosing: morning, with food, exactly per the taper
Teach infection precautions, sick-day stress dosing, and when to call
Set up monitoring (glucose, BP, weight, K) and bone protection
Reinforce a medical-alert ID and follow-up labs

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the patient's statement show correct understanding?

opts.text: "I'll take it in the morning with breakfast."
"I won't stop it suddenly — I'll follow the taper."
"I'll call if I get a fever or feel ill, and ask about a higher dose when sick."
"I'll take calcium and vitamin D and keep my bone-density follow-up."
"I'll wear a medical-alert bracelet."
"Once my pain is gone I'll just quit the pills."
"I should get the live shingles/MMR vaccine now while on steroids."
"I'll add a couple of ibuprofen daily for extra relief."

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify the exact taper schedule and have her state it back
Teach administration: morning, with food, never skip or stop abruptly
Review infection precautions, sick-day stress dosing, and when to call
Arrange monitoring (glucose, BP, weight, K) and bone-protective measures
Provide a medical-alert ID and schedule follow-up
She follows the taper and her PMR symptoms stay controlled
Glucose and BP are monitored and managed with no infection
She reports a fever promptly and gets early treatment
She takes calcium/vitamin D and keeps her bone-density follow-up
She stops prednisone on her own and becomes hypotensive, weak, and nauseated
An infection is missed because steroids blunted her fever
She develops a bleeding ulcer after adding daily NSAIDs
Unmonitored glucose climbs and she develops hyperglycemia symptoms

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
